Frontier AI agents just got a reality check in security operations.  Researchers built a threat-hunting benchmark with 106 real attack procedures, 86 MITRE ATT&CK sub-techniques, and Windows event-log databases containing 75,000 to 135,000 records per run. Five frontier models were asked to hunt malicious events with no hints. The best one found only 3.8% of them.  If you are evaluating AI for SOC workflows, this matters more than another curated Q&A benchmark. Threat hunting is open-ended, evidence-driven work. The agent has to ask the right questions, write the right SQL, and know when it has enough proof to flag an event. Current models are nowhere near reliable enough for unsupervised deployment.  The detail I would not ignore: no model cleared even a very modest bar of 50% recall across every ATT&CK tactic. The leading model managed that on 5 of 13 tactics. The other four cleared zero. That is not a small gap you patch with better prompting. It means our security evals still flatter these systems by asking cleaner questions than real work does.  Most people will read this as a limitation of LLMs in SecOps. I think it is a warning about enterprise AI evaluation in general. If the task is messy, adversarial, and requires iterative evidence gathering, single-answer benchmarks are giving leaders false comfort.  #AI #Agents #Cybersecurity arxiv.org/abs/2604.19533

AI companies love throwing around benchmark numbers, but heres one that actually matters: testing frontier models against real attack procedures from OTRF — 106 techniques, 86 MITRE ATT&CK sub-techniques, 12 tactics.  Spoiler: even the best frontier models still leave security teams hanging. The gap between impressive demo and actual threat hunting in production remains massive. These benchmarks measure something real, which is more than most AI security claims can say.

Benchmark reality check (arxiv 2604.19533): Cyber Defense Benchmark testa 5 modelos frontier (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Kimi K2.5, Gemini 3 Flash) em threat hunting real — 106 attack procedures do OTRF, 86 sub-técnicas MITRE ATT&CK, 12 tactics. Tarefa: identificar timestamp exato de eventos maliciosos em DB SQLite de 75-135k log records via query iterativa.  Resultado: TODOS falham dramaticamente. O melhor (Claude Opus 4.6) acerta apenas 3.8 por cento dos flags em média. Nenhum run de nenhum modelo encontrou todos os flags.  Definição de passing: mais de 50 por cento recall em cada tactic ATT&CK (mínimo pra deploy SOC unsupervised). O leader passa em 5 de 13 tactics. Os outros 4 modelos: zero.  Pra quem vende SOC-analyst-via-LLM como produto: o pitch não bate com bench independente. 3.8 por cento é ruído, não automation. Próxima geração precisa de hooks específicos pra security domain — vanilla frontier não entrega.  arxiv.org/abs/2604.19533

Wallet security failures are no longer edge cases, they are routine outcomes of how DeFi permissions are structured.  Most exploits today don’t “break wallets,” they exploit pre-approved access that users forget they granted or never fully understood.  In recent protocol hacks, attackers have consistently leveraged unlimited approvals, stale allowances, and complex contract interactions that bypass manual review flows.  Tools like revoke.cash and Pocket Universe add visibility, but they operate after exposure exists when risk has already been created or executed.  @CerbAgent introduces a different security layer: real-time AI agents that evaluate transaction intent, simulate outcomes, and block malicious or abnormal behavior before signature finalization.  Instead of passive monitoring, $CERB functions as an active decision layer embedded in the transaction path reducing reliance on user judgment in high-speed environments.  As on-chain activity accelerates, wallet security is shifting from manual control to intelligent automation, and $CERB is positioned directly at that transition point.

The whole thing executed without slipping, at scale, under pressure, in real time.  And it happened right in the middle of the US-Israel vs Iran ceasefire window . Brokered under maximum #geopolitical pressure, when every major market participant was watching the Strait of Hormuz, not their on-chain analytics dashboard. Lazarus Group gets the blame.  But my brain keep coming back to a different question: who actually needed the proof of concept?  Because think about it from the perspective of a large-scale capital mover, anyone who needs to move significant funds anonymously, quickly, and without leaving a trail that can be frozen mid-move. The question they always need answered is simple: can this infrastructure handle serious volume without breaking?  It just got answered. On-chain. Verifiable. Publicly documented.  This isn't me accusing anyone. I don't have the evidence for that. Intellectual honesty matters more to me than a provocative take.  What I'm saying is this: the timing is too precise, the execution too structured, and the outcome too legible to accept a single-source attribution and call it a day.  Lazarus Group may well be responsible. But "North Korea did it" has become the narrative equivalent of a closed door, and closed doors make me curious about what's behind them.  I wrote about why permissionless infrastructure like THORChain is becoming rational financial rails in a world where geopolitical instability is the new baseline. The Kelp DAO operation , whoever was behind it, just made that thesis harder to ignore.  Again, this is a theory. Not a conclusion. But it's a theory built on on-chain data, not headlines.

This is called Business Email Compromise (BEC)  and it’s one of the most expensive cyber attacks today.  No hacking tools needed.   Just psychology. Intel 471 named ClickFix the malware proliferation method of choice in 2025.  It bypasses nearly every technical email control you've paid for.  The mechanic: → Victim lands on a crafted page (malvertising, SEO, fake CAPTCHA overlay) → Page instructs: "Win+R → Ctrl+V → Enter" (or PowerShell / Terminal) → The command they paste was pre-staged by the attacker. It runs with their privileges, in their session.  No attachment for your sandbox. No URL for your filter. The payload arrives through the user's own hands.  Underground pricing confirms industrialization: full ClickFix kits now trade at $1,500–$2,000 per page.  Traditional phishing tells (typos, domain checks) don't apply. The user isn't deceived about sender identity — they're given a plausible technical instruction and asked to execute it.  Good news: cleanest decision point we've seen in years.  One key action this quarter — teach one line, everywhere:  "No legitimate website, IT team, or CAPTCHA ever asks me to paste a command into Run, PowerShell, or my terminal. If I'm being asked to do that, I stop and report it."  60-second briefing. Back of the badge. Then flip on EDR behavioral detection for clipboard → Win+R → cmd/powershell execution on non-developer endpoints. Most EDRs support it. Most orgs haven't enabled it.  One sentence. One detection. Closes the dominant 2025 delivery method.  INTEL-18 — ClickFix: When the User Becomes the Exploit.  #cybersecurity #phishing

미군, 사이버 보안 테스트 위해 비트코인 노드 운영  -미 인도태평양사령부가 실제 비트코인 노드를 운영 중  -Samuel Paparo 제독은 작업증명(PoW)을 미국의 권력 투사 도구로 평가  -현직 미군 전투사령관이 의회에서 비트코인을 국가 안보 자산으로 언급한 것은 처음 + 미국 인도-태평양 사령부에서 운영중인거 보니까 제이슨 로워리의 영향이 있는 듯.  미 인도태평양사령부는 약 38만 명의 병력을 관리하며 아시아-태평양 지역을 담당 미vs중 전략 경쟁의 핵심 전장  합의 알고리즘 PoW가 중요.  기존 사이버전은 공격 비용이 방어 비용보다 저렴해 공격자가 항상 유리한 구조.   하지만 비트코인을 공격하기 위해서는 전력, 하드웨어, 시간이 필요.  이는 미국이 비트코인을 단순한 “금융 자산”이 아니라 “전략 인프라”로 보기 시작했다는 뜻. 【"AIで守る"と"AIで襲う"が同じ道具になった日——.envファイル3万個が流出した事件と、個人開発者が今週触るべき"鍵の置き場所"】  ▍起きたこと、順番に DFIR Reportが4月22日に公開した解析で、攻撃者のサーバーから1万3千以上のファイル、150以上のディレクトリが押収され、S3バケットには4月10日〜21日の間に集められた3万個を超える.envファイル名が眠っていたことが分かった。獲物はAI、クラウド、決済、メッセージング、DBの資格情報で、確認済みの成功侵入だけで900件以上。攻撃者はこのスキャナーの設計・改善・ドキュメント化にClaude Codeを日常的に使い、Chain-of-Thoughtログにはモデルが"改善案"を考える様子まで残っていた。要するに、AIコーディング補助が攻撃側の開発速度も同じだけ引き上げてしまった現場の記録だ。  同じ週に別の研究で、Palo Alto Unit 42の「Zealot」と呼ばれる多エージェント型攻撃PoCが、"GCPから機密データを盗め"というたった一文の指示で、ネットワーク探索→Webアプリの脆弱性発見→SSRF→GCPメタデータからサービスアカウントトークン奪取→BigQueryからの抽出、までを自走で実行した。昨日まで「資料上のリスク」と呼べたものが、「再現できる攻撃チェーン」に変わっている。  ▍なぜこれが"モデル比較"より怖いか 議論は「どのモデルが賢いか」で止まりがちだけれど、今回見えたのは別の軸だ。AIコード補助の効率は、攻撃者にも等しく効く。1人の攻撃者が30万規模の.envをスキャン→ふるい分け→高価値ターゲットへ集中できるのは、AIが"退屈な運用"を肩代わりしたからにほかならない。つまりAIの進歩は、攻撃側の固定費まで引き下げてしまった。  ▍本当に解かれようとしている課題 表層のニュースは「Claude Codeが悪用された」「Zealotがやばい」だが、その裏で実際に解くべきは——開発者が普段置いている"鍵の場所"と"権限の境界"が、AIエージェント時代の前提に合っていない、という構造問題だ。.envをリポジトリ直下に置き全キーを同居させる慣習も、サービスアカウントに広めの権限を付けて"あとで絞る"と先延ばしにする運用も、AI自走攻撃の前では「1回刺されたら全部抜ける」設計になってしまう。  ▍個人開発者が今週触るべき3点 1) .envの棚卸し：本番キー・決済キー・DB資格情報は.envから外し、シークレットマネージャかOS keychainに移す。古いローカルの.envも全消しで良い。 2) サービスアカウントの最小権限化：BigQuery・S3・Stripeなど、読み取りで足りるものは書き込み権限を剥がす。SSRFを踏んでも被害範囲を限定できる。 3) 外部送信の承認ゲート：メール送信／決済／削除／依存追加は、自作エージェントでも人間承認を1段必ず挟む。便利さより「1回の誤爆で事業が止まらない」方を優先する。  ▍締め AIは"仕事を任せる相手"として成熟しつつあるが、同時に"攻撃者が雇う即戦力"としても成熟している。個人開発者のほうが大企業より動きが速い分、鍵の置き場所と権限の境界を先に整える方が、この先の信用差になる。今日30分、.envと権限だけ見直すのが、いちばん単価の高い時間の使い方だと思う。  #AI #ClaudeCode #セキュリティ #AIエージェント #個人開発 #生成AI #DevSecOps

Email spoofing still works in 2025 because DNS email security records are misconfigured — or missing entirely.  The DFIR Suite /phishing/dns endpoint gives you instant visibility into a domain's email security posture. Pass in a domain, get back its SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and MX records with security implications clearly flagged.  Real-world use: You're triaging a phishing campaign. Sender domain looks legitimate but something feels off. Hit this endpoint — 1 credit, seconds later you see the domain has no DMARC policy and a permissive SPF record. That's your confirmation: the domain is spoofable. Document it, pivot to other IOCs, and move on.  Also useful for proactive defense: audit your own organization's domains. Are your DMARC policies set to reject? Is your SPF record too broad? This endpoint surfaces the misconfigurations that attackers exploit.  Available on all plans at hXXps://platform[.]dfir-lab[.]ch/docs/phishing/dns — lightweight, structured output, built for analysts who need answers fast.  #DFIR #DFIRSuite

Recent threat intelligence highlights the exposure of “Bissa Scanner,” a modular, AI-assisted platform for large-scale web exploitation and credential harvesting, as detailed in a new report from The DFIR Report.  (1) Goals The platform is designed to scan millions of internet-facing targets, exploit vulnerabilities for initial access, harvest high-value secrets (especially .env files containing credentials), validate stolen access, and prioritize high-value victims—particularly in finance, cryptocurrency, and retail sectors—for potential follow-on data collection and operations.  (2) Architecture and Design Bissa Scanner employs a mature, modular workflow architecture that includes target acquisition via “acquirer” files, exploit assignment through “lease” files, automated data staging, and exfiltration to an S3-compatible Filebase bucket (bissapromax). It integrates AI tools such as Claude Code (for codebase analysis, troubleshooting, and workflow refinement via Chain-of-Thought prompting) and OpenClaw (a local AI control surface with websocket and browser automation). Alerting and operator interaction rely on dedicated Telegram bots (@bissapwned_bot and @bissa_scan_bot), enabling efficient triage and orchestration at scale.  (3) TTPs Tactics center on opportunistic mass exploitation, primarily leveraging the critical pre-authentication remote code execution vulnerability CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell) in React Server Components. Techniques include automated payload deployment for secret enumeration (covering cloud metadata, databases, cryptocurrency wallets, and more), batching harvested .env files into ZIP archives for upload, real-time hit scoring, and AI-enhanced review. Over 900 successful compromises were documented. A secondary WordPress module for CVE-2025-9501 showed only version-checking activity with no confirmed exploits. Data was staged locally before exfiltration, with operators focusing on validation rather than indiscriminate post-compromise actions.  This operation illustrates the growing use of AI to streamline exploitation pipelines. Organizations are encouraged to patch aggressively and implement secret management best practices.  #Cybersecurity #ThreatIntelligence #DFIR #VulnerabilityManagement #CredentialHarvesting #AIinCyber #React2Shell  References: •  The DFIR Report: “Bissa Scanner Exposed: AI-Assisted Mass Exploitation and Credential Harvesting” (April 22, 2026) – buff.ly/pxtvJIn •  NVD: CVE-2025-55182 Detail – buff.ly/74qUQIs •  Microsoft Security Blog: “Defending against the CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell) vulnerability” (December 15, 2025) •  Google Cloud Blog: “Multiple Threat Actors Exploit React2Shell (CVE-2025-55182)” (December 12, 2025) •  NVD: CVE-2025-9501 Detail – buff.ly/yqwCwEA

The investigation found no evidence that end user vault data was accessed or at risk, or that production data or production systems were compromised. Once the issue was detected, compromised access was revoked, the malicious npm release was deprecated, and remediation steps were initiated immediately.  The issue affected the npm distribution mechanism for the CLI during that limited window, not the integrity of the legitimate Bitwarden CLI codebase or stored vault data.  Users who did not download the package from npm during that window were not affected. Bitwarden has completed a review of internal environments, release paths, and related systems, and no additional impacted products or environments have been identified at this time. ‼️🇫🇷 ANTS (Agence Nationale des Titres Sécurisés / France Titres), the French government agency responsible for issuing national identity cards, passports, driving licenses, and vehicle registrations, has allegedly had additional data published on a popular cybercrime forum, labeled "Part 2" and framed as retaliation for the case being referred to the Paris Prosecutor. ⠀ ‣ Threat Actor: breach3d ‣ Category: Data Breach / Hacktivist Retaliation Release ‣ Victim: ANTS (ants.gouv.fr) ‣ Industry: Government / National Identity and Travel Documents ⠀ ANTS is the central French government authority for secured identity and travel documents, handling digital requests for passports, CNI (Carte Nationale d'Identité), driving licenses, and vehicle registration.   The agency publicly disclosed a security incident detected on 15 April 2026, and the French Ministry of Interior has since confirmed the breach affects approximately 11.7 million accounts. The case has been referred to the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), the Agence Nationale de la Sécurité des Systèmes d'Information (ANSSI), and the Paris Prosecutor, with investigations assigned to the Office Anti-Cybercriminalité (Ofac). ⠀ The actor now claims the dataset includes, in addition to the initial account leak: ⠀ ▪️ 600 million lines of data (see caveat below) ▪️ Logs and plaintext passwords ▪️ API keys and encryption keys ▪️ Source code ▪️ Database metadata and internal schema ▪️ Links to ID cards and passports in XML format ⠀ Sample artifacts visible in the post include: ⠀ ▪️ Internal Spring framework debug logs from the ANTS portal API (o.s.security.web.FilterChainProxy, RequestMappingHandlerMapping) timestamped April 2026 ▪️ XML fragments referencing permis de conduire (driving license) pre-demand transactions via predemande-permisconduire.an…, with named citizens, document types, and internal tokens ▪️ SQL SELECT statements against the internal user table with column names covering identity fields, SIRET/SIREN business identifiers, connection logs, addresses, and birth data ⠀ Caveats for interpretation: ⠀ ▪️ The 600 million line figure is materially larger than the 11.7 million accounts officially acknowledged. The Ministry of Interior has publicly stated that actors typically inflate volumes on dark web listings, and French press has flagged this specific claim as likely exaggerated. ▪️ ANTS stated in its disclosure that account passwords were not accessed and that uploaded supporting documents (scanned ID cards, etc.) did not leak. The actor's claim of "plaintext passwords" and "links to ID cards and passports" is directly contested by the agency's official position. ▪️ Officially disclosed exposed fields per user: login ID, civility, name, first names, email, date of birth, unique account ID, and optionally postal address, place of birth, and phone number. ▪️ The sample artifacts appear consistent with real ANTS backend infrastructure, but volume and content claims should not be treated as verified. ⠀ The "Part 2" framing and the actor's explicit reference to the Paris Prosecutor suggest this release is timed as retaliation for law enforcement escalation rather than a separate breach. Further releases from the same actor should be expected if investigations continue.

This is visibility into how they think.  ⸻  Q: How would you define Dark Storm Team today, hacktivists, cybercriminals, or something else?  A: We see ourselves as a group of like-minded individuals. Today, many label all hackers as cybercriminals, but we consider ourselves hacktivists driven by ideological motivations.  ⸻  Q: What differentiates you from groups like KillNet or Anonymous-affiliated actors?  A: It’s better to judge us by effectiveness. Operations targeting major platforms already demonstrate our level.  ⸻  Q: Can you walk us through a recent operation?  A: We won’t disclose operational details to avoid strengthening defenses. In general, we rely on a mix of social engineering, intelligence gathering, and specialized tooling.  ⸻  Q: How do you ensure your claims are credible?  A: Verification is challenging due to limited reporting, but independent cybersecurity experts have validated some of our activities.  ⸻  Q: What are your primary attack methods?  A: A combination, including DDoS, exploitation, and credential access.  ⸻  Q: Tools, public, private, or custom?  A: All of the above. We use open-source tools, external resources, and our own developments.  ⸻  Q: How is your team structured?  A: There is leadership, but members can change. Alignment in mindset is key.  ⸻  Q: What role do ideology or geopolitics play?  A: A major role. Our operations are heavily influenced by political and ideological perspectives.  ⸻  Q: Are defenders getting better?  A: It varies. Strong security requires investment, not every organization prioritizes it.  ⸻  Q: Most challenging targets?  A: Time constraints, defensive countermeasures, and resource limitations.  ⸻  Q: How do you manage OPSEC?  A: We focus on minimizing digital footprints and maintaining strict operational discipline.  ⸻  Q: How do you respond to skepticism?  A: Skepticism is natural. Attribution and impact are often difficult to prove publicly.  ⸻  Q: Do you collaborate with others?  A: Yes. We work with other groups, including newer alliances like “Armenian Code.”  ⸻  Q: Biggest misconception about your group?  A: That our operations are ineffective.  ⸻  Q: If organizations want to avoid being targeted, what should they do?  A: (Response reflects their viewpoint), targeting decisions are influenced by multiple factors, including perceived alignment and impact.  ⸻  Audience Q: Do you choose targets based on vulnerability or symbolic value?  A: We prioritize targets based on potential impact, then identify the path to reach them.  ⸻  Audience Q: How do DDoS attacks impact large tech companies?  A: The objective is to bypass defenses or identify weaker parts of infrastructure.  ⸻  Final Note (DDW): This interview reflects the group’s claims and perspective. Attribution in cyber operations remains complex, and not all claims can be independently verified.  ⸻

I need to scream into the void of the internet. I don't expect any of you to give a shit. I just want to share an observation I've had.  Since I've had a kid I've noticed some parents unironically ego others about their children. I'm not sure what it is, maybe it's some sort reflection of their personalities, but I think it's super fucking weird.  Like, my son was an objectively large baby. He was born in the 100th percentile, meaning he is statistically larger than 100 percent of babies. This doesn't mean he's destined to be a freak athlete, it doesn't mean he's going to be walking giant, it means almost nothing because the cause of large babies is either the Mother is diabetic ... or it's genetics. In our case it was genetics. It's totally possible he will be large now and by the time he's 10 he will be BELOW average. The size of a baby at birth doesn't reflect anything about their future.  When I tell someone my son was born large, I'll always have one person interject and say something akin to "MY SON WAS BIGGER" ... even though I know they're lying or exaggerating.  Alternatively, I'll say something like, "I think my son is doing really good, he eats a lot". Suddenly some parent will be like "MY DAUGHTER EATS MORE. SHE EATS EVERYTHING".  I've seen parents blatantly LIE about their children's abilities. I've had parents tell me their children began walking completely unassisted at 5 months old. That sort of development is virtually impossible. Babies at that age do not possess the coordination skills to walk yet. LITERALLY their BRAIN isn't physiologically PREPARED for movement of that sort yet. Early-early-EARLY walking babies is like, 7 months old. However, this is freakishly rare, it's an anomaly.  I've seen parents exaggerate or lie about: - Height - Weight - Eating habits  - Coordination  - Intelligence  - Learning speed - Academic skills - ???  It's a really, really, really weird one-up thing with their children. I have no idea why people do it.  I've also noticed some parents AGGRESSIVELY try to shove their ideas down your throat and assert THEY ARE right and YOU are wrong about literally anything.  It's the weirdest shit bro. I get it, you love your children and you're proud of them, but they turn it into a competition. God damn

I'm a big fan of old ass shit discussing religion. I'm an Atheist, but I respect the impact religion has brought to this planet and how it's shaped our culture.  I've been reading and researching the Divine Comedy (Dante's Inferno, Purgatory, and Paradiso). I initially began reading the original text (closest possible English translation) but it was really, really, really challenging. I didn't know what the fuck bro was yapping about.  Part of the issue is weird grammatical structure and archaic vocabulary, but also the pop-culture references from the year 1321 (release date, but technically was written much earlier, whatever).  I decided then to find a book that has "updated" verbage and a break down of what's actually being discussed. I ended up settling on "The Inferno" by John Ciardi  Ciardi and others spent years translating the original poem. He and his colleagues traveled to Italy and met with Italian historians and academics to break down the text further to really emphasize the "pop-culture" references and demystify what Dante Alighieri was yapping about.  As I was trying to find a book to read on it, I found dozens of AI slop "books" on The Divine Comedy. The introduction unironically contains shit like, "Why The Divine Comedy Matters" and uses arrows to break down segments.  AI slop in a book for sale makes me physically sick to my stomach. John Ciardi and others spent years of their lives working to convey the literature masterpiece the Divine Comedy is, and some fuckin schmuck tries to AI slop it and sell it on Amazon.  I hate it so much it's unreal

최근 위협 환경은 공개된 취약점(CVE)이 발표된 지 13시간 이내에 즉각적으로 무기화되는 'Zero-Day/N-Day' 공격의 가속화가 두드러집니다. 특히 AI 모델을 활용한 취약점 탐색 및 공격 자동화 시도가 증가하고 있으며, 공급망 공격(Supply Chain Attack)을 통해 개발자 자격 증명을 탈취하는 정교한 캠페인이 지속되고 있습니다. 또한, 공격자들이 가정용 공유기나 IoT 기기를 공격 은폐를 위한 프록시 네트워크로 악용하는 사례가 국제적인 보안 경고의 핵심으로 부상했습니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
- **UNC6692**: Microsoft Teams를 통해 IT Helpdesk로 위장하여 사용자에게 접근, 커스텀 악성코드 제품군을 배포하는 사회공학적 기법 사용 (Mandiant Blog).
- **Tropic Trooper**: 일본의 타겟과 가정용 공유기를 주요 공격 대상으로 삼으며, 도구와 TTP를 빠르게 변화시키는 중국 연계 APT 그룹 (Dark Reading).
- **GopherWhisper**: 몽골 정부 기관을 타겟으로 활동하는 신규 중국 연계 APT 그룹 (WeLiveSecurity).
- **TeamPCP**: Bitwarden CLI 및 NPM 패키지를 타겟으로 한 공급망 공격 캠페인 수행 (SecurityWeek).
- **0APT**: 경쟁 랜섬웨어 조직인 Krybit의 내부 자료를 탈취하고 협박하는 범죄 조직 간 해킹전 양상 (데일리시큐).

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
- **기업뱅킹 소프트웨어(WGear) 취약점**: 금융보안원 및 KISA를 통해 기업뱅킹 전자금융 서비스의 대용량 처리 프로그램 취약점 대응 공지 (이스트시큐리티 알약).
- **국내 APT 공격 동향**: 2026년 3월 기준, 국내 타겟 대상 스피어 피싱 이메일 및 .lnk 파일 기반의 파워쉘 실행 공격이 주를 이룸 (AhnLab ASEC).

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
- **CVE-2026-3844**: Breeze Cache WordPress Plugin, CVSS 9.8(Critical), 인증되지 않은 원격 파일 업로드 및 사이트 탈취 가능, 현재 악용 중.
- **CVE-2026-21643**: Fortinet FortiClient EMS 7.4.4, CVSS 9.8(Critical), 사전 인증 SQL 인젝션 및 RCE 가능, 현재 악용 중.
- **CVE-2026-33626**: LMDeploy, High-severity, LLM 배포 툴킷 SSRF 취약점, 공개 13시간 만에 악용 시작.
- **CVE-2026-32201**: Microsoft SharePoint Server, Actively exploited, CISA 긴급 패치 대상.
- **CVE-2026-28950**: Apple Notification Services, iOS/iPadOS 취약점, 삭제된 알림 데이터 복구 가능, 악용 사례 확인.

### 5. IOC 요약
- **CVE**:
    - CVE-2026-33626 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/lmdeploy-cve-2026-33626-flaw-exploited.html))
    - CVE-2026-28950 ([SANS ISC](https://isc.sans.edu/diary/rss/32922))
    - CVE-2026-6074 ([CISA ICS Advisories](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/ics-advisories/icsa-26-113-06))
    - CVE-2026-6375, CVE-2026-6376 ([CISA ICS Advisories](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/ics-advisories/icsa-26-113-04))

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
- **Breeze Cache 및 FortiClient EMS 점검**: 해당 제품 사용 시 즉시 최신 버전으로 패치하고, 비정상적인 파일 업로드 로그나 웹 GUI 접근 이력을 모니터링하십시오.
- **공급망 보안 강화**: 개발 환경에서 사용 중인 NPM 패키지(특히 Bitwarden CLI 관련)의 무결성을 검증하고, 의심스러운 의존성 업데이트를 차단하십시오.
- **공유기 및 IoT 기기 보안**: 기업 네트워크 내부의 가정용 공유기나 IoT 장비가 외부와 직접 통신하지 않도록 격리하고, 최신 펌웨어 업데이트를 적용하십시오.

### 7. 위협 수준 평가
- **점수**: 8.5/10
- **근거**: 공개 취약점의 무기화 속도가 매우 빠르고, 공급망 공격과 APT 그룹의 활동이 동시다발적으로 발생하여 기업 인프라에 대한 실질적인 위협이 매우 높은 상태임.
